findings.description: Nested <main> landmark elements - ensure the document has at most one main landmark at top level
findings.evidence: <main> at line 27 contains another <main> at line 35. Only one <main> landmark is valid per document.

findings.description: The DEQ logo in the HTML is positioned differently (top-left corner) compared to the PDF (top-center), and appears to be a slightly different rendering of the logo
findings.evidence: PDF: Logo centered at top of page with 'NORTH CAROLINA Department of Environmental Quality' text beneath. HTML: Logo in top-left corner, smaller, without the full text beneath it.

findings.description: The Comments section in the PDF has a visible bordered text box for input, while the HTML version has no visible box/border for the comments area
findings.evidence: PDF: Comments section has a clearly visible rectangular bordered box below it. HTML: Comments section has no bordered input area.

findings.description: The checklist items in the PDF have 'YES' and 'NA' as column headers with checkboxes implied below them, while the HTML uses two empty square checkboxes (□ □) before each item
findings.evidence: PDF: Items listed under 'YES NA (not applicable). Check all that apply:' with implied checkbox columns. HTML: Each item preceded by '□ □' characters representing the two checkboxes inline.

findings.description: The document title is split across h1 and h2, with 'Inactive Hazardous Sites Branch' as h1 and 'Annual Report Form for Perpetual Land-Use Restrictions' as h2. In the PDF, both lines together form the document title/header. While acceptable as a structural choice, the 'Property Owner Certification Statement' is rendered as h3, creating an h2→h3 relationship that doesn't reflect the PDF's flat structure where it's a bold section heading at the same organizational level.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'Inactive Hazardous Sites Branch' and 'Annual Report Form for Perpetual Land-Use Restrictions' are the document title. 'Property Owner Certification Statement' is a bold section heading. HTML uses h1, h2, and h3 respectively.

findings.description: The HTML has nested <main> elements — there is an outer <main> from the body structure and an inner <main> wrapping the form content. This is a structural duplication of the main landmark element, though it does not duplicate visible content.

The first checklist item in the PDF has only one checkbox column (YES only, no NA), while the HTML shows two checkboxes (☐ ☐) for that item, same as all other items. In the PDF, the first item 'All restrictions in the recorded Declaration of Perpetual Land Use Restrictions (DPLUR) document are still in compliance' only has a YES checkbox with no NA checkbox visible.
findings.evidence: PDF image: First checklist item has only one checkbox (YES). HTML: '☐ ☐ All restrictions in the recorded Declaration of Perpetual Land Use Restrictions (DPLUR) document are still in compliance.'
